Tragacanth, derived from the plant genus Astragalus, is a type of gum or adhesive obtained from the sap of certain species of Astragalus plants. It has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties, which can help to treat digestive issues and skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Tragacanth include polysaccharides, flavonoids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Tragacanth is commonly used to prepare topical creams, ointments, and wound dressings, as well as oral suspensions and syrups, to treat various health conditions.

This classification places Tragacantha serenoi within the larger group of legumes, which are characterized by their fruit pods and ability to fix nitrogen from the atmosphere. The genus Tragacantha is further grouped within the subfamily Faboideae, which includes a wide range of plants commonly known as vetches and clovers. Tragacantha serenoi's classification is part of the broader system of plant taxonomy, which aims to categorize and understand the relationships between different species.
